content.text: WSGI and ASGI are the two Python specifications that define how a web server hands off HTTP requests to application code. Choosing between them is one of the first architectural decisions when building an agent API: it controls whether streaming LLM tokens, WebSockets, and concurrent tool calls are possible at all, and it determines the scaling ceiling before infrastructure costs dominate.
(Web Server Gateway Interface, PEP 3333) is a synchronous Python standard introduced in 2003. It defines a callable contract:
. The server calls that function once per HTTP request, passes a dictionary of request data as

, and receives the response body as a return value. The worker process is occupied — blocked — for the entire duration of that call. Flask, Django (pre-async), and Bottle are WSGI frameworks; Gunicorn and uWSGI are WSGI servers.
(Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface, PEP 3492) is the async successor ratified in 2019. It replaces the simple function call with an async triple:
is an async callable that yields incoming messages;

dispatches outgoing messages. Crucially, while an awaited operation is pending, the event loop is free to handle other requests. FastAPI and Starlette are ASGI frameworks; Uvicorn and Hypercorn are ASGI servers.

Both WSGI and ASGI are protocol standards, not libraries. Any compatible server can run any compatible framework. This separation of concerns means an operator can switch from Gunicorn to uWSGI or from Uvicorn to Hypercorn without touching application code.

An agentic AI service has a fundamentally different I/O profile from a traditional CRUD API. A single agent request may await 3-5 sequential LLM calls, each taking 5-30 seconds; it may fan out to a dozen tool HTTP calls in parallel; and it may stream thousands of tokens back to the client as they arrive. Under WSGI, every second spent waiting for the LLM is a second the worker is idle but unavailable to other requests. Under ASGI, those idle seconds are lent to concurrent requests sharing the same event loop.

The difference is not theoretical. A WSGI server with 8 workers handling 30-second agent runs can serve at most 8 concurrent users. An ASGI server with 8 workers and async I/O throughout can serve hundreds of concurrent users whose waits overlap on the event loop. Streaming LLM token output — emitting each word as it arrives rather than waiting for the full completion — is simply impossible through standard WSGI; ASGI's

Choosing the wrong interface has cascading consequences: WSGI teams that outgrow their worker count add expensive RAM-heavy processes and still hit concurrency limits; teams on ASGI who accidentally call a blocking library bring their entire event loop to a halt. Understanding both specifications at the protocol level prevents both failure modes.
Simple mental model — one request per worker, no async/await, no event-loop pitfalls
Mature operations — decades of Gunicorn and uWSGI production knowledge, tutorials, and default configs
Universal library compatibility — every Python library works without async wrappers or thread-pool workarounds
Easy debugging — synchronous stack traces are linear; no need to understand coroutine scheduling
Predictable resource usage — memory footprint scales exactly with worker count, no coroutine overhead
Fine for low-throughput internal agent triggers where traffic never exceeds worker count
True async I/O — hundreds of in-flight LLM and tool requests per single worker process via coroutine multiplexing
Streaming HTTP bodies and SSE as first-class primitives, not hacks requiring chunked-encoding tricks
WebSocket scope — persistent bidirectional connections for real-time agent interactions and cancellation signals
Lifespan events — startup and shutdown hooks for shared async database pools, model connections, and message brokers
HTTP/2 support in Hypercorn, enabling multiplexed streams over a single TCP connection
Lower memory footprint under concurrent load because coroutines share a single OS thread stack
Naturally expresses parallel tool calls using asyncio.gather without spawning threads
— simplest to write, but each worker idles while awaiting the model; scale by adding processes at high memory cost (300-600 MB per process)
— a single synchronous DB call or time.sleep() in an async route blocks the entire event loop, degrading every concurrent request on that worker
— some older SDKs are sync-only; wrapping them with asyncio.to_thread() adds overhead and complexity
— coroutine tracebacks can be harder to read; race conditions in shared mutable state require care
— mounting a WSGI sub-app inside ASGI via Starlette's WSGIMiddleware is possible but adds overhead and limits streaming
— embedding generation and reranking are CPU-bound; neither WSGI nor ASGI helps without ProcessPoolExecutor or a dedicated microservice
— async Python requires understanding coroutines, event loops, and awaitable objects; mistakes are silent without load testing
When a client sends an HTTP request, the WSGI server (Gunicorn) selects an idle worker process from its pool, populates the

to declare response status and headers, then returns an iterable of byte strings as the body. The server sends them to the client and the worker becomes available. The worker is entirely blocked during view execution — network waits translate directly to worker exhaustion.
ASGI wraps the HTTP lifecycle in async message passing. The server (Uvicorn) calls
. To read the request body the app calls
; to send a response chunk it calls

yields control back to the event loop, which can interleave other coroutines. A response streaming 500 LLM tokens over 10 seconds occupies the event loop only during the fractions of time spent sending bytes; the rest is available to other requests.
ASGI defines a third scope type:
. When the server starts, it sends a
message. The application can open shared async resources — database pools, shared
instances, model connections — and hold them for the server's lifetime. On shutdown, a
message fires, allowing graceful cleanup. FastAPI exposes this through its

# WSGI (conceptual - Flask/Gunicorn)
def application(environ, start_response):
    status = "200 OK"
    headers = [("Content-Type", "application/json")]
    start_response(status, headers)
    return [b'{"ok": true}']  # worker blocks until this line

# ASGI (conceptual - FastAPI/Uvicorn)
async def application(scope, receive, send):
    if scope["type"] == "http":
        # receive() yields control; other coroutines run while waiting
        event = await receive()
        await send({
            "type": "http.response.start",
            "status": 200,
            "headers": [(b"content-type", b"application/json")],
        })
        # more_body=True enables streaming; False on last chunk
        await send({
            "type": "http.response.body",
            "body": b'{"ok": true}',
            "more_body": False,
        })
The diagram below contrasts the full WSGI and ASGI request paths from the reverse proxy through to the application, including the worker model, protocol support, and typical deployment commands.

attrs.alt: ASGI vs WSGI request flow diagram

content.text: In the WSGI path each worker is a separate OS process, blocked for the duration of the handler. In the ASGI path a single Uvicorn worker runs an asyncio event loop that interleaves many concurrent coroutines. The WSGI stack can approximate streaming via chunked responses but cannot handle WebSocket connections; the ASGI stack supports HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket natively through scope type discrimination.
The agent service is a short internal trigger with low concurrent traffic (fewer than 5 simultaneous requests)
The codebase already uses Flask or Django and migration cost exceeds concurrency benefit
The team is unfamiliar with async Python and correctness is higher priority than throughput
The service is purely CPU-bound where async I/O provides no throughput advantage
The API streams LLM tokens to clients via SSE or WebSocket
Agent runs invoke multiple tool HTTP calls that can be parallelized with asyncio.gather
Real-time bidirectional communication is required (live agent progress, user cancellation signals)
The service must handle hundreds of concurrent long-lived agent sessions
Shared async resources (HTTP client pools, Redis connections) need lifespan management

For any new agentic service, default to ASGI with FastAPI and Uvicorn. The mental overhead of async Python is justified by the scalability gains for I/O-bound agent workloads. Migrate existing WSGI services when streaming or high concurrency becomes a bottleneck.
Below is a complete ASGI agent API demonstrating lifespan resource management, a non-streaming JSON endpoint, an SSE streaming endpoint, and the correct pattern for wrapping synchronous SDKs.

# requirements: fastapi uvicorn httpx
# Run: uvicorn agent_api:app --reload

import asyncio
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncIterator, AsyncGenerator

import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel

# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Lifespan: open/close a shared async HTTP client for all tool calls
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI) -> AsyncIterator[None]:
    # startup - runs once before the first request
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient(
        timeout=httpx.Timeout(60.0),
        limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=50),
    )
    try:
        yield  # server is running; handle requests
    finally:
        # shutdown - runs after the last request
        await app.state.http.aclose()


app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)


class AgentRequest(BaseModel):
    message: str


# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Async JSON route - awaits LLM without blocking other requests
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
@app.post("/agent/invoke")
async def invoke_agent(req: AgentRequest) -> dict:
    try:
        response = await app.state.http.post(
            "https://api.example.com/v1/chat",
            json={"model": "gpt-4o", "messages": [
                {"role": "user", "content": req.message}
            ]},
            headers={"Authorization": "Bearer $API_KEY"},
        )
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.json()
    except httpx.HTTPStatusError as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=502, detail=str(e))


# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Streaming SSE route - yields tokens as they arrive
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
async def _token_stream(message: str) -> AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    # In production, iterate over an async SDK streaming response:
    # async for chunk in async_client.messages.stream(...):
    #     yield f"data: {chunk.text}\n\n"
    words = f"Agent reply to: {message}".split()
    for word in words:
        yield f"data: {word}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # yield event loop between tokens
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"


@app.post("/agent/stream")
async def stream_agent(req: AgentRequest) -> StreamingResponse:
    return StreamingResponse(
        _token_stream(req.message),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable nginx buffering
        },
    )


# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Wrapping a synchronous SDK - asyncio.to_thread avoids loop block
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
def _legacy_sync_tool(query: str) -> str:
    """Simulates a blocking third-party SDK (e.g. old vector DB)."""
    import time
    time.sleep(2)
    return f"legacy result for: {query}"


@app.get("/tool/legacy")
async def call_legacy(query: str) -> dict:
    # asyncio.to_thread submits to the default ThreadPoolExecutor,
    # returning control to the event loop while the thread runs.
    result = await asyncio.to_thread(_legacy_sync_tool, query)
    return {"result": result}
The bare WSGI contract — no framework — to show exactly what Gunicorn calls and what the application must return:

# wsgi_app.py
# Run: gunicorn wsgi_app:application -w 4
import json

def application(environ, start_response):
    """Minimal WSGI callable. Gunicorn calls this once per request."""
    method = environ.get("REQUEST_METHOD", "GET")
    path   = environ.get("PATH_INFO", "/")

    if method == "POST" and path == "/agent":
        length  = int(environ.get("CONTENT_LENGTH", 0) or 0)
        body    = environ["wsgi.input"].read(length)
        data    = json.loads(body) if body else {}
        result  = {"echo": data.get("message"), "interface": "WSGI"}
        payload = json.dumps(result).encode()
        start_response("200 OK", [
            ("Content-Type", "application/json"),
            ("Content-Length", str(len(payload))),
        ])
        return [payload]   # worker was blocked until this return

    start_response("404 Not Found", [("Content-Type", "text/plain")])
    return [b"Not found"]
Parallel tool calls with asyncio.gather under ASGI
This example shows the signature advantage of ASGI for agentic workloads — running three tool HTTP calls concurrently from a single async route:

# parallel_tools.py  (FastAPI / ASGI)
import asyncio
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

async def fetch_tool(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient, tool_url: str, payload: dict
) -> dict:
    resp = await client.post(tool_url, json=payload)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

@app.post("/agent/parallel-tools")
async def parallel_tools(body: dict) -> dict:
    """
    Calls three tools in parallel.
    Total latency = max(tool latencies), not sum - the ASGI advantage.
    """
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        search_task  = fetch_tool(
            client, "https://tools.internal/search",
            {"q": body["query"]}
        )
        weather_task = fetch_tool(
            client, "https://tools.internal/weather",
            {"city": body["city"]}
        )
        cal_task     = fetch_tool(
            client, "https://tools.internal/calendar",
            {"date": body["date"]}
        )
        search, weather, calendar = await asyncio.gather(
            search_task, weather_task, cal_task
        )
    return {
        "search": search,
        "weather": weather,
        "calendar": calendar,
    }

# Under WSGI this would require three sequential HTTP calls,
# tripling latency: if each tool takes 2s, total = 6s.
# Under ASGI with asyncio.gather, total latency = ~2s (slowest tool).

A production multi-agent platform started on Flask (WSGI) with Gunicorn, 16 worker processes. Each agent run held a worker for 20-40 seconds during sequential LLM calls. At 16 concurrent users the service queued requests; at 30 users it became unusable. Memory usage per worker was 400 MB, so 16 workers cost 6.4 GB of RAM for a ceiling of only 16 concurrent sessions.
After migrating to FastAPI (ASGI) with Uvicorn (4 workers), the service used

(opened in the lifespan context) for all outbound HTTP. Total RAM dropped to 1.8 GB (4 x 450 MB). Observed concurrency increased from 16 simultaneous sessions to over 200, because the 20-40 seconds of LLM I/O wait is now free for other coroutines. SSE streaming was added with no architectural change — just a
generator. The legacy Django admin panel remained on a separate Gunicorn process group, kept isolated because it has no streaming or high-concurrency requirements.
Blocking I/O inside an async route
blocks the event loop; replace with
Assuming more Gunicorn workers is the answer
— each worker is a full OS process; 32 workers x 400 MB = 12.8 GB RAM and still only 32 concurrent sessions
— WSGI has no WebSocket scope; the request/response model cannot represent persistent bidirectional connections
— nginx buffers upstream responses by default; omitting
causes nginx to swallow SSE tokens and deliver them in batches
— each Gunicorn worker is an independent process; module-level Python dictionaries are not shared and will diverge silently
CPU-bound work on the ASGI event loop
— embedding generation or image processing in an async route blocks all other coroutines; offload to
Creating a new httpx.AsyncClient per request
— skips connection pooling, causes TLS handshake overhead, and exhausts file descriptors under load; open one client in lifespan and reuse it
returns a coroutine object that is never scheduled; no error is raised, but the call has no effect
Default all new agent APIs to FastAPI + Uvicorn (ASGI); justify WSGI only when there is a specific reason such as an existing codebase or sync-only library dependency
Open shared async resources (HTTP clients, DB pools, Redis connections) in the lifespan context, not at module import time or inside route handlers
Audit every route for blocking calls; use
in nginx for all SSE or WebSocket endpoints
Load-test with realistic agent workloads — long hold times and concurrent sessions — not synthetic RPS benchmarks
Keep WSGI services that cannot be migrated isolated behind a task queue so they never block agent-facing ASGI routes
Instrument event loop lag in production to detect accidental blocking code; log warnings when a callback holds the loop for more than 100 ms
for multi-core deployment; N = 2 x CPU cores + 1 is a common starting point, then tune from load tests

WSGI throughput scales linearly with worker count. Adding a worker adds one concurrent session at the cost of one full process (300-600 MB RAM). The ceiling is (workers) / (average_latency_seconds) requests per second. For 30-second agent runs and 8 workers that ceiling is approximately 0.27 RPS — a dangerous limit for any public endpoint.

ASGI throughput scales with the I/O-wait fraction of each request. If 90 percent of an agent request is network I/O (awaiting LLM or tools), a single worker can theoretically overlap 1 / (1 - 0.9) = 10 concurrent requests. In practice, with four Uvicorn workers on a 4-core machine, 100-400 concurrent agent sessions with 15-second average hold times is achievable. RAM stays proportional to worker count, not session count, because coroutines require a few KB each versus hundreds of MB per OS process.

The performance boundary shifts for CPU-heavy work. Embedding generation, reranking, and running small local models are CPU-bound; neither WSGI nor ASGI helps. The asyncio event loop runs on a single OS thread per worker; CPU-bound Python code holds the GIL and starves the event loop. Use
submitted from an async route for these operations, or move them to a dedicated microservice called via async HTTP.
Connection pool sizing matters under ASGI. An

caps outbound HTTP connections; set this deliberately or async fan-out from many concurrent coroutines can exhaust file descriptors. A rule of thumb: max_connections = (max concurrent requests per worker) x (max simultaneous tool calls per request).
— most common WSGI server; supports sync, gthread, eventlet, and gevent worker types; robust process management
— feature-rich with harakiri timeout, response caching, and built-in routing; common in enterprise Django deployments
— pure-Python, cross-platform; used in Windows deployments where Gunicorn is unavailable
— built on uvloop + httptools; fastest ASGI server for most workloads; standard for FastAPI
— supports HTTP/1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC); useful when HTTP/2 multiplexed streaming matters
— Django Channels' reference ASGI server; supports HTTP and WebSocket
— Starlette-based; automatic OpenAPI docs, Pydantic validation, dependency injection, lifespan hooks; standard for new agent APIs
— the ASGI toolkit underneath FastAPI; lightweight, composable middleware, WebSocket support, WSGIMiddleware for phased migration
— Django 3.1+ supports async views and ORM under Daphne or Uvicorn; allows incremental migration of existing Django apps
— high-performance ASGI framework with strong typing, OpenAPI support, and built-in dependency injection
— async HTTP client; drop-in replacement for requests in async context; supports SSE streaming and connection pools
— async database drivers; avoid blocking SQL calls under ASGI without run_in_executor workarounds
— async Redis client; useful for distributed rate limiting, session state, and pub/sub in streaming pipelines
— structured concurrency abstraction over asyncio and trio; used internally by Starlette and Uvicorn
The table below compares WSGI, ASGI, Celery background workers, and gRPC — the most common patterns for building agent service APIs:
High — full OS process per worker
Low — event loop shares idle time

Celery and ASGI are complementary. A common production pattern uses ASGI for the synchronous request/response and streaming paths, with Celery or ARQ for background tasks that exceed HTTP timeout limits — multi-hour agent runs or batch embedding jobs.
Long-running agent sessions make timeout configuration critical. Under WSGI, uWSGI's
setting kills a worker that exceeds a time limit; Gunicorn uses
. Under ASGI, set explicit timeouts on the async HTTP client (
guard to prevent runaway agent loops from holding connections indefinitely.
) to prevent file descriptor exhaustion under traffic spikes
Apply rate limiting at the ASGI middleware layer before agent logic runs — per-IP or per-API-key limits
in Uvicorn to restart workers periodically, preventing memory leaks from long-lived async clients accumulating state
Use a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) to terminate TLS, enforce request body size limits, and absorb slow-client attacks before they reach the application layer
header; LLM API keys and agent tokens appear there and would be exposed in log aggregation pipelines
Validate authentication in a middleware layer applied before routing to guarantee uniform enforcement across all routes
Avoid passing user-supplied content directly to shell commands or OS paths inside WSGI/ASGI workers; agent inputs can contain prompt injection payloads
ASGI lifespan's shutdown event enables graceful draining: stop accepting new work, wait for in-flight coroutines to finish, then close connection pools. Gunicorn's
sequence provides similar phased shutdown under WSGI. Always configure a graceful shutdown timeout to avoid dropping in-progress agent sessions during rolling deploys.

Picture a bank with tellers (workers). WSGI is a bank where each teller takes one customer from the queue and handles them start to finish — including the 20 minutes the customer spends filling out a form. The teller stands idle during that wait but no other customer can use them. The queue grows as more customers arrive.

ASGI is a bank where tellers can say 'fill out this form and come back; I'll help someone else while you write.' Each teller handles many customers whose paperwork is pending. The same four tellers serve hundreds of customers efficiently because idle waiting is no longer the bottleneck.

The practical corollary: in ASGI, blocking the teller (running synchronous code in an async route) is far worse than in WSGI because it stops the entire branch, not just one lane. The async model demands discipline — every wait must be an awaitable, or the performance advantage collapses.
WSGI (PEP 3333) is synchronous — one blocking call per request per worker; suitable for low-concurrency internal services or existing Flask/Django codebases where migration cost is high
ASGI (PEP 3492) is asynchronous — one event loop handles many concurrent coroutines; required for LLM token streaming, WebSockets, parallel tool fan-out, and high-concurrency agent APIs
ASGI's scope / receive / send protocol natively supports HTTP, WebSocket, and lifespan events — WSGI models only HTTP request/response pairs
Never call blocking I/O directly inside an ASGI async route; use
for unavoidable sync or CPU-bound code
Open shared async resources (HTTP clients, DB pools) in the ASGI lifespan context to enable connection reuse across requests and clean shutdown when the server stops
Deploy ASGI with Uvicorn; deploy WSGI with Gunicorn; always place a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) in front for TLS, body size limits, and buffering control
Load-test with realistic agent workloads — long hold times and concurrent sessions — not synthetic RPS benchmarks; the I/O-wait fraction determines the effective concurrency multiplier
